title: Sh. Om Prakash Seth vs Sh. Aman Ghai & Anr on 4 January, 2008
body: Sh. Om Prakash Seth vs Sh. Aman Ghai & Anr on 4 January, 2008 -:1:- IN THE COURT OF SH. SATISH KUMAR ARORA, CIVIL JUDGE, DELHI EX NO : 77/07 Sh. Om Prakash Seth ..........Plaintiff Versus Sh. Aman Ghai & Anr. ..........Defendants ORDER 1. By way of this order, I propose to dispose of the issue as to the maintainability of the present execution petition, as raised by the ld. counsel for the Judgment Debtors/respondents. Brief facts necessary for the adjudication of the present issue may be summarized as under. 2. Decree Holder (plaintiff in the suit no. 1344/06) filed the present execution petition against the judgment debtors (defendants in the suit no. 1344/06) stating therein that in a suit for perpetual injunction filed by the decree holder against the judgment debtors, the judgment debtors have given a Page 1 of 10 -:2:- statement on oath on 01.11.2006 to the following effect "We undertake that while demolishing the first floor of the suit property, we shall not cause any damage to the ceiling/roof of the premises under the tenancy of the plaintiff. In case the roof over the passage in question marked A, B, C, D in the site- plan filed by the plaintiff (Ex. P-1) is demolished by the defendants, then the plaintiff shall be entitled to affix ACC Sheets/tin-shed/fiber shed over the portion A, B, E, F at the same height and only for prevention of water logging. The defendants shall not interfere with the common use of the passage, A, B, E, F by the plaintiff. The defendants shall not interfere with the ingress and egress of the plaintiff over the portion C, D, E, F in the site-plan Ex. P-1 for the purpose of cleaning, repair of sewage and water pipes/water meter." On the aforesaid statement of the judgment debtors (defendants therein), the decree holder/plaintiff made the following statement "I have heard the statement of defendant no. 1 & 2. My suit may be disposed of in terms of undertaking given by the defendants.". It is further stated that after recording the respective statements of the defendants and the plaintiff, the suit was disposed of by the Ld. Predecessor by order dated 01.11.2006 on the following terms "Statement of defendants recorded on SA. Statement of Page 2 of 10 -:3:- plaintiff also recorded on SA. In view of the statement of the plaintiff and defendants, present suit is disposed of as settled. Parties shall be bound by their respective statements. File be consigned to Record Room." 3. It is the case of the decree holder that the judgment debtors have willfully failed to obey the order dated 01.11.2006 and therefore the said order is to be enforced by attachment of the property of the judgment debtors and by their detention in civil prison. Further, the decree holder also prays for the relief of restoration of the roof of the premises in dispute to its original condition by repair of the same through a local commissioner to be appointed by the court. Decree holder also prays for breaking open the lock placed on the main gate so that the decree holder is able to have ingress and egress to the premises through the passage. 4. Pursuant to the service of the notice of the present execution petition, the judgment debtors by filing a reply had prayed for dismissal of the present execution petition on the following terms :- (i) Since no decree was passed by the Ld. Court of Sh. Sanjeev Kumar Page 3 of 10 -:4:- Singh, Ld. Civil Judge, Delhi (my Ld. Predecessor), the present execution petition is not maintainable and is liable to be dismissed; and (ii) That the judgment debtors/respondents have not in any manner violated their statement made on oath on 01.11.2006 in the court. 5. The decree holder by filing the rejoinder to the reply as filed by the judgment debtors, states that the present execution petition is very well maintainable for the reason that on mere perusal of the order dated 01.11.2006, it is clear that the suit was disposed of and the rights of the parties to the suit were finally determined. It is further stated that it is the nature of the order and not the mere presence or absence of a formal expression which determines whether the order is executable or not. It is further stated that in view of the provisions of Order 21 Rule 32 R/w Section 36 CPC, the final orders passed in any suit can be executed by the process of execution. As to the averments made by the judgment debtors that they have not violated their statement made on 01.11.2006, the same is denied by the decree holder. 6. I have heard the respective submissions of the ld. counsel for the Page 4 of 10 -:5:- parties and perused the record carefully. Ld. counsel for the J.D.'s contends that the order dated 01.11.2006 for which the execution petition is filed is not having any semblance of the decree so as to call for its execution as per the provisions under Section 36 CPC. Reference can be made to Section 36 of the Code of Civil Procedure which falls under Part II of the Code dealing with "Execution". It lays down "the provisions of this code relating to the execution of decrees (including provisions relating to payment under a decree) shall, so far as they are applicable, be deemed to apply to the execution of orders (including payment under an order)." Ld. counsel for the J.D.'s contends that not all orders are executable as the same is reflected in the use of the expression. "So far as they are applicable" under Section 36 CPC. Ld. counsel for the J.D.s further contends that with respect to the order dated 01.11.2006, the remedy available to the decree holder is to initiate the contempt proceedings against the alleged disobedience of the said order and not the initiation of the execution proceedings. In his support, ld. counsel for the J.D.s places reliance on the authorities cited in AIR 2002 Bombay 487 and AIR (29) 1942 Bombay 97. I have perused both the authorities and the same are not applicable to the facts in hand. Whereas the authority cited in AIR Page 5 of 10 -:6:- 2002 Bombay 487 dealt with the aspect of applicability of provisions of Section 36 CPC to the orders passed under Order 39 Rule 1 & 2 CPC . It was observed that the orders passed under Order 39 Rule 1 & 2 CPC are of temporary nature and can operate until disposal of the suit or until further orders. As to the authority cited in AIR (29) 1942 Bombay 97, it dealt with the issue as to whether when the District Judge, acting under a scheme of management framed by a court, passes an order for cost, can the said order be executed. It was held that when he is acting under a scheme of management framed by a court, he is not acting in a judicial capacity and consequently, the order for cost made by him though legal is not capable of execution under the Code of Civil Procedure . 7. Ld. counsel for the decree holder, on the other hand, contends that the order dated 01.11.2006 is capable of execution in the light of provisions as contained in Order 21 Rule 32 R/w Section 36 CPC. Ld. counsel contends that Order 21 Rule 32 CPC which lays down the mode of execution of the decrees for specific performance, for restitution of conjugal rights and for injunction is fully applicable to the facts of the present case by virtue of Page 6 of 10 -:7:- Section 36 CPC. Order 21 Rule 32 CPC inter-alia lays down - Where a party against whom a decee for an injunction has been passed, has had an opportunity of obeying the decree and has willfully failed to obey it, the decree may be enforced by his detention in the civil prison, or by the attachment of his property, or by both. In his support, ld. counsel for the D.H. places reliance on the authorities cited in (2006) 9 SCC 222 and AIR 2006 Supreme Court 1883. In the authority cited in (2006) 9 SCC 222, the issue before the Hon'ble Supreme Court was whether the High Court has erred in giving the direction to the appellants (respondents before the High Court) to remove the debris after demolishing the construction put up by the appellants subsequent to the undertaking given by them to the trial court on 31.10.1994. Brief facts in this case were - That in a suit filed by the plaintiff/respondent in the Court of Additional Senior Sub-Judge, Ajnala, seeking permanent injunction restraining the defendants/appellants permanently from dispossessing the respondent or from interfering with his peaceful possession in the suit property, the defendant/appellants gave an undertaking by making a statement on oath before the court on 31.10.1994, and the suit was dismissed as infructuous by order of the same date directing the defendants/appellants to Page 7 of 10 -:8:- remain bound by their statement. Subsequent to the decision in the suit, the plaintiff/respondent filed an application under Order 21 Rule 32 CPC with the prayer that the appellants had violated the undertaking given in the suit filed by him and therefore the property of the appellants be attached and sold and, in case they fail to abide by the undertaking, they be sent to civil prison. In the authority cited in AIR 2006 Supreme Court 1883, it was observed "that all decrees and orders are executable under the Code of Civil Procedure . Consent decrees or orders are of course also executable." 8. The question which is now to be determined is - As to whether the order 01.11.2006 is non-executable having no attributes of an order as per the provision of Section 36 CPC, or is executable in the light of the provisions as contained in Order 21 Rule 32 R/w Section 36 CPC? The order dated 01.11.2006 is reproduced here in verbatim : - "Statement of defendants recorded on SA. Statement of plaintiff also recorded on SA. In view of the statement of the plaintiff and defendants, present suit is disposed of as settled. Parties shall be bound by their respective statements. File be consigned to Page 8 of 10 -:9:- Record Room." From the bare reading of the order, it is clear that it is finally determining the suit of the plaintiff as settled, on the terms as recorded in the respective statement of the parties. It can be said that the aforesaid order falls within the provisions as contained in Order XXIII of the Code of Civil Procedure , and can be said to be a 'consent order'. The said order was made upon the settlement/agreement of compromise arrived at between the parties. The terms of settlement/agreement are delineated in the respective statements of the defendants and the plaintiff. Thus, the order dated 01.11.2006 firstly, recorded the settlement between the parties and secondly, mandated the parties to abide by the terms of the settlement. Once there is a mandate to abide the terms of settlement as recorded in the statement of defendants and plaintiff, the order could be executed, in case any of the parties allege that the other party has willfully disobeyed the order. Further, merely because a decree has not been drawn up, will not mean that the said order is not executable. Further, merely because the remedy of initiating contempt proceedings is available to the party, it can in no manner estop the party from initiating the execution proceedings. Page 9 of 10 - : 10 : - Thus, the contentions of the ld. counsel for the J.D.'s that the order dated 01.11.2006 is not having any semblance of a decree and is thus non- executable, is untenable and is rejected accordingly. As to the contention of the ld. counsel for the J.D.'s that the execution petition is not maintainable for the reason that there is no violation of order dated 01.11.2006 by the J.D.'s it is to be decided on the basis of evidence, to be led by the parties. However, from the photographs placed on record by the D.H., a prima-facie opinion could be formed as to the violation of order dated 01.11.2006. For the foregoing reasons, I hold that the present execution petition is maintainable with respect to the order dated 01.11.2006 in the light of the provisions as contained in Order 21 Rule 32 R/w Section 36 R/w Order 23 Rule 3 CPC . Matter be listed for consideration on the application under Order 26 Rule 9 R/w Order 21 Rule 32 CPC and for further proceedings, for 11.04.2008. Announced in open court: Dated : 4th January, 2008 (SATISH KUMAR ARORA) (Three copies attached) CIVIL JUDGE, DELHI Page 10 of 10
